This is the 2nd launch from Cosyra. View more

## Cosyra 2.0

Launching today

A cloud workspace for coding agents from your phone

Cosyra gives you a persistent workspace for cloud agents. Your repos, branches, files, dependencies, terminals, and agent work stay together. Open it from your phone and keep building without leaving a laptop awake.

Free Options [...] Hey Product Hunt, Adam here, co-founder of Cosyra. I started building Cosyra because I kept running into the same annoying problem. I’d give a coding agent a task, walk away from my laptop, and come back later to discover it had been waiting for an answer the whole time. I wanted the agent in my pocket instead. Our first version put the terminal on your phone. After watching our first 50 developers use it, we realized the terminal wasn’t the most important part. The persistent workspace was. Cosyra v2.0 is a platform for your coding agents that you access from your phone. Your Ubuntu workspace keeps your repos, branches, files, installed dependencies, terminal sessions, and agent work together. Close the app, come back later, and continue where you left off. Your laptop doesn’t need to [...] continue where you left off. Your laptop doesn’t need to stay awake. You can still work through a full terminal, connect GitHub, switch between sessions, and preview localhost apps. We also added a chat experience for people who are less comfortable living in the terminal. You can try Cosyra free for up to 10 hours over 7 days. I’ll be here throughout launch day answering questions. I’d especially love to hear how you currently keep coding agents moving when you’re away from your desk, and what Cosyra would need before you’d trust it with your own workflow. Thanks for checking out Cosyra v2.0

results.title: Codex 3.0 by OpenAI Reviews (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
used Codex 3.0 by OpenAI to build Nugget

•1 review

This was a project that I built for OpenAI build week. That was part of the requirements that you use Codex and GPT 5.6 but I also use Claude Code, which is not on this project. That said I'm quite impressed with Codex and GPT-56 on this project. It went way smoother than similar projects I've done.

#### Alternatives Considered

Report

Cosyra 2.0

used Codex 3.0 by OpenAI to build Cosyra 2.0

•5 reviews

I just really like this CLI. Open AI is also super generous with usage, which we really needed when building Cosyra. My favorite thing about Codex is probably its tiny footprint. Since it's built in Rust, it's just far more compact and snappier than its counterparts in OpenCode and Claude Code.

#### Alternatives Considered

Report [...] Vizhi

used Codex 3.0 by OpenAI to build Vizhi

•2 reviews

Vizhi actually grew out of an earlier tool I built for Claude Code (claude-console). For OpenAI Build Week I rebuilt the idea around Codex CLI, and it carried the whole project two ways. Codex with GPT-5.6 wrote nearly all the code — TypeScript, C#, Swift, and a bit of AppleScript. Codex's lifecycle hooks are what make Vizhi's live session grid possible at all.

#### Alternatives Considered

Report

Nugget

used Codex 3.0 by OpenAI to build Nugget

•1 review [...] #### vs Alternatives

CursorCursorClaude CodeClaude Code

Been using Claude Code and Cursor heavily. Claude is great at reasoning through complex problems but it has a habit of assuming things about your code instead of checking. Cursor is fast for inline edits but less useful for multi-file tasks. Codex fills a different gap, it's the one I trust most to go off and do a chunk of work autonomously without coming back with code that references things that aren't there.

Terminals [...] ### Best for

Ideal for developers who want an open-source, customizable agent in their existing IDE or terminal workflow.

### Standout features

 ✓Open-source and workflow-adaptive design
 ✓Supports open models for a FOSS stack
 ✓Runs inside IDE terminals
 ✓Customizable agent harness behavior
 ✓Fits alongside existing tooling

## Cosyra

CosyraCosyra

Run AI coding agents from your phone

Learn more →

Cosyra is differentiated by mobility: it’s built to make a cloud terminal and AI-dev workflows practical from a phone, not just a laptop. That makes it a meaningful alternative to Warp when access and responsiveness on the go matter more than a desktop-grade terminal UI. [...] Instead of treating mobile as a remote desktop compromise, Cosyra focuses on running agent CLIs directly with parallel sessions via tabs and side panels. This enables quick context switches between ongoing tasks—useful for on-call scenarios, monitoring long-running agent jobs, or kicking off fixes while away from the workstation.

The experience is geared around terminal multiplexing in a mobile-friendly format, emphasizing session management and continuity. The trade-off is that it’s less about reinventing the desktop terminal and more about extending agent-driven development into moments where a laptop isn’t available. If the need is to keep agent sessions and command-line workflows accessible anywhere, Cosyra is the most purpose-built option in this set.

# CosyraAlternatives

Looking beyond Cosyra? Explore Warp for a fast, open-source ADE, Claude Code for deep-context coding, and Cursor for agentic IDE help. On mobile, try Vibe Pocket or MobileCLI to run AI agents and control terminals on the go.
